content:
Web Development

JavaScript

Aviation

5 min read
Just now

--

Today’s AeroType update focused on improving the project in three important areas: local image synchronization, a more polished aircraft detail UI, and higher-quality aircraft data.
AeroType is not only a simple list of aircraft models. My goal is to make it feel like a practical aircraft dictionary where users can visually recognize aircraft families, read their key characteristics, and understand detailed specifications in a clear and enjoyable way.
With today’s update, AeroType became more complete both visually and informationally.

Replacing Remote Images with Local Project Images
One of the main improvements was replacing unstable remote image links with local image files stored directly inside the project.
Previously, some aircraft images relied on external sources such as Wikipedia links. While this worked in some cases, remote image links can become unstable, fail to load, or display inconsistently depending on the environment.
To solve this, I replaced those images with locally selected aircraft photos and synchronized them into the project’s images/ directory.
The newly added or updated images include:

• A321neo

• Boeing 777X family

• Boeing 787–9 family

• Bombardier CRJ family

• De Havilland Dash 8 family

• Embraer ERJ family

• Embraer E-Jet family

• Embraer E-Jet E2 family

• McDonnell Douglas MD-11 family

• McDonnell Douglas MD-90 family
After adding these files, I updated the imageUrl values in data/aircrafts.js so each aircraft entry now points to a local project image.
This change makes AeroType more stable because the aircraft images are no longer dependent on external links. It also gives me more control over which aircraft photos appear in the dictionary.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Adding Photo Captions to Aircraft Detail Modals
Another visible improvement was the new photo caption area inside the aircraft detail modal.
In many cases, an aircraft family contains several variants. For example, a Boeing 737 NG family page may include a photo of a specific variant such as the 737–800. Without a caption, users may not immediately know which exact model is shown in the photo.
To make this clearer, I expanded the aircraft data model by adding a new photoVariant property to all 36 aircraft entries.
For example:

• b737-ng now displays the photo variant as B737-800

• b767-family now displays the photo variant as B767-300 (non-ER)
I also updated the modal rendering logic in script.js. When an aircraft has an image and a photoVariant value, the modal now displays a caption such as “Photo of B737-800.” If photoVariant is missing, the system falls back to the original aircraft model name to preserve compatibility.
This small UI improvement makes the aircraft detail page much clearer, especially for users who want to understand the difference between aircraft families and specific variants.

Improving the Modal Design
The new photo caption area was also styled carefully to match the overall AeroType design.
In styles.css, I added styling for the .modal-image-caption area. The caption uses a soft background color, a calm gray text tone, and centered alignment to make it feel naturally integrated into the modal.
The goal was not just to add more information, but to make the information feel clean and visually consistent.
This update helps the modal feel more like a polished aircraft information card rather than a simple technical data window.

Adding Description Sections for All Aircraft Types
The biggest content improvement was the addition of aircraft descriptions.
Until now, AeroType mainly focused on structured specifications such as seating capacity, range, first flight year, and production status. These details are useful, but they do not fully explain the character or identity of each aircraft.
To improve this, I added a new section below the variant specification table:
“Aircraft Introduction and Key Features”
This section provides a short Korean description for each aircraft type. All 36 aircraft entries now include a description explaining the aircraft’s history, role, identity, or distinctive features.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Simple description of Boeing 747This makes AeroType feel much closer to an actual aircraft dictionary. Instead of only showing numbers, it now gives users context.
For example, I refined the A220 description to include its strong economic performance and its unique Pratt & Whitney engine sound, which is sometimes described as a whale-like resonance during takeoff or landing.
I also improved the Boeing 747 description by emphasizing its iconic upper-deck shape and historical importance as one of the most recognizable passenger aircraft ever built.
These descriptions help users understand not only what the aircraft is, but also why it matters.

Adding the Boeing 737–900(non-ER) Variant
I also improved the specification accuracy of the Boeing 737 NG family.
Previously, the 737 NG variant list did not separately include the original Boeing 737–900 non-ER model. To make the data more complete, I added the 737–900 as a separate variant alongside the 737–900ER.
The newly added 737–900 data includes:

• Type name: 737–900

• Max seating: 189 passengers

• Range: 5,080 km

• First flight: 2000

• Production status: Retired
This distinction is important because the original 737–900 and the later 737–900ER have meaningful differences, especially in capacity and operational design. Adding the non-ER version makes the 737 NG data more accurate and complete.

What This Update Means for AeroType
Today’s update was not about adding one large new feature. Instead, it was about improving the overall quality of AeroType.
The project now has more stable local images, clearer aircraft photo captions, better modal design, richer aircraft descriptions, more consistent terminology, and more accurate variant data.
These improvements make AeroType feel more complete as an aircraft dictionary. Users can now not only view aircraft specifications, but also understand the identity and characteristics of each aircraft family more naturally.
For me, this update was also meaningful because it reminded me that a good service is not only built through big features. Small details such as image reliability, captions, terminology, and data accuracy can greatly improve the overall experience.
AeroType is gradually becoming more than a simple aircraft list. It is growing into a more polished, informative, and visually engaging aircraft reference service.
